The Client Capture Crash Course

Database Reactivation: The Money Sitting in Your Old Lead List

You will learn how one short text to people who already know you can fill a slow week without spending a dollar on ads.

The Problem

Somewhere in your phone, your email, or a drawer of old estimates is a list of people who contacted you, got a quote, or hired you once, and then went quiet. Every one of them cost you money to acquire: ad spend, time on the phone, a site visit.

Most owners treat that list as dead. It is not. People who did not buy were often just not ready. The kitchen remodel got postponed. The budget cycle reset. The water heater limped along another year.

Meanwhile, when business slows down, the default move is to spend more on ads to find strangers. That is the most expensive possible way to get a job, while a list of warm names sits untouched.

Reactivation is the highest-return campaign in this entire course because the hard part, getting someone to know who you are, is already done.

The Workflow

How It Works, Step by Step

  1. 1

    Pull together the old list

    Past customers, old quotes, dead leads, no-shows from last year. It does not need to be organized. Names and phone numbers are enough.

  2. 2

    One personal-sounding text goes out

    Not a blast that screams marketing. Something like: "Hey, it's Mike from Mike's Plumbing. We did your water heater estimate last spring. Clearing the schedule for next month and thought of you. Still on your list?" Sent in small batches so replies stay manageable.

  3. 3

    Replies come back, more than you expect

    Some say not yet. Some unsubscribe. And a meaningful slice say "actually, yes, glad you texted." These are people who already trusted you enough to call once.

  4. 4

    The replies become booked jobs

    Every yes goes straight into your normal booking conversation. No ad spend, no stranger danger, no price shopping against five competitors. Just a warm lead you had been sitting on the whole time.

What This Looks Like in Real Life

A lash tech has a slow February coming. She has 240 past clients in her booking history, about 60 of whom have not been in for over four months. One Tuesday morning, a reactivation text goes out to that lapsed list while she works on a client. By the end of the week she has 14 replies and 9 bookings, an entire week of revenue, from people whose numbers were already in her phone.
